2606.23633.title: AI Exposure Scores: what they measure, what they miss, and what comes next
2606.23633.paper_summary: A set of exposure scores calculated in 2023 has become a central empirical input to the future of work debate. Produced by Eloundou et al. (2023) and referred to here as the GPTs are GPTs scores, they define exposure as the share of occupational tasks a large language model can assist with. This work is a genuine methodological contribution, but as the scores travel from the time and place they were produced, the limitations the authors named do not always travel with them. Two gaps have widened as a result. The first is structural, between what static exposure scores measure and what policy questions actually require. Taking the diffusion of these scores as a case study, we show how their temporal, geographic, and ontological limitations compound in policy-facing analyses, and we survey five families of research responding to these limits: dynamic and benchmark-based measures, ensemble methods, task-framework extensions, worker-centered metrics, and adoption and usage data. The second gap is the one we argue needs more attention: the coordination between researchers and policymakers. The policy-relevant work which ask who is harmed, who benefits, how, and when, continues to reference the static GPTs are GPTs scores without engagement with the methodological updates that would let these questions be answered more reliably. We then ask what additional steps towards navigating uncertainty remain: ex-post frameworks and the deliberate, political work of reimagining what futures are worthy of building towards are. Closing the research-policy gap is a shared task: policymakers must widen their evidence base, engage workers as epistemic partners, and shift from prediction to preparedness; researchers must build data infrastructure, adopt participatory methods, and write with policymakers in mind. Better measurement matters, but it will not close the second gap alone.
2606.23633.paper_summary_zh: 一組在2023年計算的暴露分數已成為未來工作辯論的核心實證輸入。由Eloundou等人（2023）製作，這裡稱為GPTs的GPTs分數，將暴露定義為大型語言模型可以協助的職業任務的比例。這項工作是一個真正的方法論貢獻，但隨著這些分數從其產生的時間和地點傳播，作者所提到的限制並不總是隨之而來。結果出現了兩個擴大的差距。第一個是結構性差距，存在於靜態暴露分數所測量的內容與政策問題實際需求之間。以這些分數的擴散為案例研究，我們展示了它們的時間、地理和本體論限制在面向政策的分析中如何相互加劇，並調查了五個研究家族以應對這些限制：動態和基準測量、集成方法、任務框架擴展、以工人為中心的指標以及採用和使用數據。第二個差距是我們認為需要更多關注的：研究人員與政策制定者之間的協調。與政策相關的工作詢問誰受到傷害、誰受益、如何以及何時，仍然繼續參考靜態GPTs是GPTs分數，而不參與方法論更新，這些更新可以讓這些問題更可靠地得到回答。我們接著詢問，還有哪些額外步驟可以用來應對不確定性：事後框架和重新想像值得建設的未來的故意政治工作。縮小研究與政策之間的差距是一項共同的任務：政策制定者必須擴大其證據基礎，將工人視為認識上的夥伴，並從預測轉向準備；研究人員必須建立數據基礎設施，採用參與式方法，並以政策制定者為考量進行寫作。更好的測量很重要，但僅靠這一點無法單獨縮小第二個差距。
2606.23633.author: Campbell Lund et.al.
2606.23633.authors: Campbell Lund, Thomas Euyang, Zanele Munyikwa, Marzieh Fadaee
